real_meaning: This pattern expresses that you have had the experience of doing something at least once in your life — it's about existence of past experience, not about recency or habit. The speaker is essentially saying 'this event exists in my personal history.' It carries a slight sense of 'it happened at some undefined point before now,' which is why it often comes up in conversations about life experiences, bucket lists, or getting-to-know-you topics.

formation.rule: Attach ことがある to the verb's plain past (た-form). Works the same for all verb types: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べた＋ことがある → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたことがある；<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>った＋ことがある → <ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>ったことがある；した＋ことがある → したことがある。Negate with ことがない or ことはない. Past tense of ある (ことがあった) shifts meaning to 'there was a time when...'
formation.common_mistake: Using the dictionary (present) form instead of the た-form: ✗ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べることがある (this actually means 'there are times when I eat...' — a completely different grammar point!). ✓ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたことがある ('I have eaten...' / 'I have had the experience of eating...')

sentences.jp: <ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>登<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>ったことある？
sentences.translation: Have you ever climbed Mt. Fuji?
sentences.notes: ことが gets contracted to ことin casual speech — totally natural and very common among friends; the particle が is typically dropped.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どものころ、<ruby>海外<rt>かいがい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>住<rt>す</rt></ruby>んだことがあります。
sentences.translation: I lived abroad when I was a child.
sentences.notes: The vague timing (<ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どものころ) is typical — the pattern focuses on the fact that the experience exists, not exactly when it happened.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>技術<rt>ぎじゅつ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>以前<rt>いぜん</rt></ruby>にも<ruby>検討<rt>けんとう</rt></ruby>されたことがあります。
sentences.translation: This technology has been considered before as well.
sentences.notes: In formal or written contexts, たことがある is used with passive forms to note historical precedent — shows the pattern isn't limited to personal experience.

sentences.jp: <ruby>死<rt>し</rt></ruby>にそうになったことがある。
sentences.translation: There was a time I almost died.
sentences.notes: Attaching ことがある to a compound form like 〜そうになった surprises learners — as long as the verb phrase ends in た-form, ことがある can follow and the meaning holds naturally.

confused_with.contrast: These two patterns look almost identical but express totally different things. ～たことがある (past + こと) is about life experience — 'I have done X at least once.' ～ることがある (dictionary form + こと) means 'there are sometimes occasions when X happens' — it's about recurring possibility or occasional occurrence, not past experience. Minimal pair: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>会<rt>あ</rt></ruby>ったことがある (I have met him before — once in my life) vs. <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>会<rt>あ</rt></ruby>うことがある (I sometimes run into him / there are occasions I meet him). Choose たことがある when you're vouching for an experience existing in your history; choose ることがある when you're describing an intermittent pattern or possibility.

confusion_set.use_when: <ruby>人生<rt>じんせい</rt></ruby>で<ruby>少<rt>すく</rt></ruby>なくとも<ruby>一度<rt>いちど</rt></ruby>その<ruby>経験<rt>けいけん</rt></ruby>があると<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>いたいとき
confusion_set.example: <ruby>馬<rt>うま</rt></ruby>に<ruby>乗<rt>の</rt></ruby>ったことがある。

confusion_set.use_when: ある<ruby>行動<rt>こうどう</rt></ruby>が<ruby>時々<rt>ときどき</rt></ruby>・まれに<ruby>起<rt>お</rt></ruby>きると<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>いたいとき
confusion_set.example: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>授業<rt>じゅぎょう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>休<rt>やす</rt></ruby>むことがある。

confusion_set.use_when: <ruby>昔<rt>むかし</rt></ruby>よくやっていた<ruby>懐<rt>なつ</rt></ruby>かしい<ruby>習慣<rt>しゅうかん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>振<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>り<ruby>返<rt>かえ</rt></ruby>るとき（<ruby>郷愁<rt>きょうしゅう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>気持<rt>きも</rt></ruby>ち）
confusion_set.example: <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どものころ、よくここで<ruby>遊<rt>あそ</rt></ruby>んだものだ。

bunpro_tip: Lock in the た-form requirement by memorizing this contrast as a pair on BunPro: たことがある = 'experience EXISTS in history' (past tense → finished event → stored memory), ることがある = 'occurrence EXISTS sometimes' (present tense → ongoing pattern). When you see a prompt about life experiences, bucket lists, or firsts, that's your cue to reach for た.
